planting.note: Transplant nursery stock in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Dig a hole twice the root width and plant at the original depth in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Give full sun to keep the creamy-gold banding bright and provide steady water, as this cultivar dislikes drought. It grows into a large pyramid prized for its striped foliage rather than for cones.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained ground.

directions: Site this dwarf in full sun to light shade with the crown level with the surrounding soil. Its long, thread-like weeping shoots form a rounded mound, so avoid crowding and keep it evenly watered. Foliage bronzes attractively in winter cold and greens again in spring.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring in cool, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant at grade in fertile, well-drained soil sheltered from harsh drying winds. The lemon-scented foliage develops best color in full sun with consistent moisture. This broadly conical tree grows slowly and produces small woody cones once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set this slow, cone-shaped dwarf in full sun to bring out its amber-gold color, deepening to coppery tones in winter. Keep the root flare at soil level and water regularly until well rooted. Light shearing in late spring keeps the compact form dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in evenly moist soil.

directions: Plant this naturally rounded golden globe at grade in full sun, where the outer foliage turns the brightest yellow. Provide steady moisture and avoid dry, exposed sites that scorch the tips. It needs little pruning to hold its neat ball shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: This very slow, tidy globe is ideal for foundations and small beds; plant it with the crown at soil level. Give full sun to partial shade and even moisture, mulching to keep roots cool. Its rich green sprays hold color well and rarely need trimming.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this miniature bun-shaped selection at grade in full sun for the densest emerald foliage held in vertical sprays. Keep it evenly watered and mulched, as its shallow roots dry quickly. It is compact enough for rock gardens and containers and needs no shaping.

planting.note: Space as a screen in spring or fall in fertile, moist soil.

directions: Set plants at grade and space them evenly for a dense hedge or windbreak. This broad pyramid holds dark green color through winter and tolerates cold better than many cultivars, but water it well during establishment. Shear lightly if a formal face is wanted.

planting.note: Plant as a screen in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this tall, narrow pyramid at grade and space closely for a living privacy wall. It keeps deep green color all winter rather than bronzing, and rewards deep watering during dry spells. Prune only to maintain a tidy face, since it naturally holds a dense column.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in evenly moist soil.

directions: Plant this slow, slender column at grade where a narrow accent or small hedge is needed. Its tightly held, rich green foliage resists winter bronzing and needs little pruning to stay neat. Give full sun and consistent moisture for the densest growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this rounded, wide-spreading globe at grade in full sun for the fullest green foliage. Heavy snow can splay the branches, so brush accumulations off and shear lightly to keep the ball tight. Water deeply through the first seasons to speed establishment.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Give this compact globe full sun so the tips flush bright red in spring and turn coppery in fall. Plant at grade in well-drained soil and water steadily, as stressed plants lose their color. No shaping is needed to keep its neat rounded outline.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this narrow, upright golden selection at grade in full sun to keep the new growth glowing yellow. Its slim profile suits tight spaces and vertical accents with minimal pruning. Keep roots evenly moist to prevent tip browning in dry weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set this dense, dwarf globe at grade in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil. It naturally forms a tidy ball of medium-green foliage that seldom needs pruning. Water regularly during establishment and mulch to conserve soil moisture.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this broad golden pyramid in full sun so the bright yellow tips stay vivid, deepening to orange in cold weather. Keep the crown at grade and water deeply until established. Light spring shearing thickens the cone and intensifies the new gold flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun for the brightest gold on the cascading, thread-like shoots that mound into a broad pyramid. Keep the root ball at grade in moist, acidic soil and water regularly, as this cultivar resents drying out. Prune only to shape, cutting back into green wood to avoid bare spots.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, acidic soil with some afternoon shelter.

directions: Plant at grade in moist, fertile, acidic soil with protection from harsh afternoon sun that can scorch the soft foliage. Its steel-blue, feathery juvenile needles form a dense cone that responds well to light shearing. Keep the soil consistently moist and mulched for best color.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained acidic soil.

directions: Set this large, billowing selection at grade in full sun to light shade in evenly moist soil. Its soft, silvery blue-green foliage has a mossy texture and forms a broad pyramid over time. Provide steady water and light shaping to keep the outline dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained acidic soil.

directions: Plant this slow, mounding dwarf in full sun to keep the drooping, thread-like foliage a bright golden yellow. Set it at grade in moist, acidic soil and water regularly to prevent tip scorch. Occasional light trimming keeps the mop-like habit tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained acidic soil.

directions: Plant this softly mounded, thread-leaved selection in full sun for glowing gold foliage that resists sunburn better than similar cultivars. Set it at grade in moist, acidic soil and keep it well watered. Its graceful weeping texture needs little pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained acidic soil.

directions: Plant this broad golden pyramid in full sun, which brings the richest yellow to the outer fronds. Keep the crown at grade in fertile, moist, acidic soil and shelter it from drying winds. It grows slowly and holds elegant layered foliage with minimal pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained acidic soil.

directions: Plant this graceful dark-green pyramid at grade in full sun to light shade in humus-rich, acidic soil. Its glossy, slightly twisted sprays give a fine texture with little care beyond steady watering. Shelter from cold, drying winds preserves the deep color.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained acidic soil.

directions: Plant in full sun for the brightest gold on the arching, fern-like fronds that give this upright shrub its name. Keep the root ball at grade in moist, acidic soil and water consistently. Its loose, graceful habit needs only occasional shaping.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained acidic soil.

directions: Plant this slow, semi-dwarf selection at grade in full sun to light shade in rich, acidic soil. Its layered, cupped sprays take on bronze tints in winter and green up again in spring. Keep the soil evenly moist and prune only lightly to preserve the natural tiered look.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this narrow, upright column at grade in moist, well-drained soil with full sun to partial shade. Its dense, feathery blue-green foliage takes a purplish cast in cold weather. Avoid waterlogged sites, which invite root rot, and shear lightly to keep the slim form neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at grade in cool, consistently moist, well-drained soil, ideally where summers are not extremely hot. Seed needs cold, moist stratification before sowing to break dormancy. This large native develops drooping branchlets and small round cones as it matures into a tall pyramid.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this dramatic weeper at grade in cool, moist, well-drained soil with full sun to light shade. Its upswept branches and long, curtain-like drooping foliage make a striking specimen, so give it open room. Keep roots moist and mulched, and avoid hot, dry, exposed sites.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant this iconic narrow column at grade in full sun and sharply drained soil, avoiding wet feet. Once established it is quite drought tolerant, so water deeply but infrequently. Its slim, dark-green form needs no pruning and bears rounded woody cones with age.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant this blue-tinged, narrow columnar form in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil. It shares the drought tolerance of the species once rooted, so avoid overwatering. The slim, upright silhouette suits formal accents and needs essentially no pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant this compact, slow-growing columnar cypress at grade in full sun and sharply drained soil. Its dense blue-green foliage and narrow form fit small gardens and large containers. Water sparingly once established and skip pruning, as it holds a tight pillar naturally.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil, ideally in mild coastal climates.

directions: Plant at grade in full sun and well-drained soil, giving this large tree ample room to spread. It thrives in mild, breezy coastal conditions and tolerates salt spray well. Water through establishment, after which it is fairly drought tolerant and bears large woody cones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil, or grow in a container in colder zones.

directions: Plant this bright golden, lemon-scented cypress in full sun for the most vivid color and a dense, narrow cone. Set it at grade in well-drained soil and water regularly, especially in containers. In cold-winter regions grow it in a pot and shelter it from hard frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in hot, dry, well-drained sites.

directions: Plant at grade in full sun and fast-draining, even poor soil, since this cypress excels in heat and drought. Water to establish, then let it rely largely on rainfall. It forms a broad blue-green to silvery pyramid and produces round woody cones with age.

planting.note: Plant in spring in hot, well-drained sites.

directions: Plant this silvery blue selection in full sun and sharply drained soil for the coolest foliage color. It is heat- and drought-tolerant once established, so avoid overwatering and heavy, wet ground. The narrow, upright form makes a striking specimen with little pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in hot, well-drained sites.

directions: Plant this fast-growing, blue-gray cypress in full sun and well-drained soil for a quick screen or specimen. Its feathery, aromatic foliage tolerates heat and drought once rooted, so water sparingly. Give it room to develop a broad pyramid and prune only to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in mild climates.

directions: Plant at grade in full sun and deep, well-drained soil in frost-free or nearly frost-free regions. This vigorous tree has gracefully drooping, gray-green branchlets and grows quickly with regular water. Give it plenty of space, as it becomes a large pyramidal specimen bearing small round cones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, frost-free spot with good drainage.

directions: Plant this elegant weeping cypress at grade in full sun to light shade in a warm, sheltered, frost-free site with good drainage. Its long, blue-gray, pendulous sprays are its main ornament, so give it open space to display them. Protect young plants from wind and cold, and water regularly.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in mild climates.

directions: Plant at grade in full sun and well-drained soil, favoring warm regions with mild winters. This tall, columnar-to-pyramidal cypress has fine, aromatic green foliage and grows steadily with regular water. Give it establishment care, then reduce watering as it becomes drought tolerant.

planting.note: Space along a hedge line in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this fast-growing hybrid at grade in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing plants for a quick privacy screen. It puts on rapid height, so shear regularly to keep a dense hedge and avoid crowding that invites disease. Water well while establishing and provide good air circulation.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil, spacing for a screen.

directions: Plant this golden-foliaged Leyland in full sun to keep the new growth a bright yellow that holds well through the year. Set it at grade in well-drained soil and shear regularly to build a dense, colorful screen. It is somewhat slower and more compact than the green form.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this broad, mounding form at grade in full sun to light shade in moist, acidic soil. Its long, drooping green thread-like shoots give a soft, weeping texture in a rounded pyramid. Keep the soil evenly moist and prune only into green wood to shape.

planting.note: Set out in autumn or early spring while the soil is cool and moist.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the same depth the tree grew in its container, keeping the root flare at the surface. It tolerates heavy shearing, so shape hedges in late spring after new growth firms. All parts except the fleshy red aril are poisonous, so site it away from grazing animals.

planting.note: Transplant in spring or fall into cool, evenly moist ground.

directions: Give it a spot with good drainage; soggy roots are the quickest way to lose a yew. It endures more cold and shade than the English species, making it a reliable foundation or hedge plant. Prune to size in early summer and keep berries away from children and pets.

planting.note: Install container or balled stock in spring or early fall.

directions: This hybrid of English and Japanese yew is usually grown from rooted cuttings for uniform hedges. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water through the first two summers until established. Shear once or twice a season to hold a formal shape.

planting.note: Best sited in cool, shaded woodland conditions in fall or spring.

directions: Native to the Pacific Northwest understory, it prefers shade and consistently damp, humus-rich soil. Growth is slow, so choose a permanent spot and mulch to keep roots cool. Seeds need long cold stratification, so expect uneven, delayed germination.

planting.note: Plant in shaded, moist ground in spring or fall.

directions: This low, sprawling native yew forms a groundcover mound in cold northern woodlands. Give it dappled shade and cool, acidic soil, avoiding hot, dry exposures. It is extremely cold hardy and needs little pruning beyond removing stray shoots.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall in a sheltered, partly shaded site.

directions: Grow this Asian yew in fertile, well-drained soil with protection from harsh afternoon sun. It responds well to shaping and makes a dense evergreen screen over time. Keep the soil evenly moist during dry spells to prevent needle browning.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock in spring or early fall in well-drained ground.

directions: This upright, columnar yew is propagated from cuttings to keep its narrow habit. Plant it as a formal vertical accent and, if desired, tie the branches loosely to prevent snow from splaying the crown. Little pruning is needed beyond tidying stray shoots.

planting.note: Space along a line for a narrow hedge in spring or fall.

directions: A narrow, upright selection prized for tall, columnar hedges in tight spaces. Plant in well-drained soil and shear the sides to keep the profile crisp. It tolerates urban conditions and moderate shade with ease.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a low, rounded foundation shrub.

directions: This compact, spreading yew stays low and wider than tall, ideal for foundations and low hedges. Set it in well-drained soil and avoid wet feet, which cause root rot. A single shearing each early summer keeps it dense and neat.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall for a broad, pyramidal hedge.

directions: A broadly pyramidal, dense yew that shears into handsome formal hedges. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly the first two years. Its upright branching resists snow damage better than spreading forms.

planting.note: Plant as a low, wide-spreading groundcover in spring or fall.

directions: This low, wide selection drapes gracefully over walls and banks with horizontal, spreading branches. Give it well-drained soil and let it sprawl, pruning only to control width. It is one of the hardier and more shade-tolerant English yew cultivars.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a pyramidal specimen or screen.

directions: This broadly pyramidal form of Japanese yew makes a bold evergreen accent or screen. Plant in well-drained soil and it will grow slowly into a full, conical shape. Shear the leader if you wish to restrain its ultimate height.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a bright spot to keep foliage gold.

directions: A narrow, slow, columnar yew with golden-yellow foliage that colors best in full sun. Its slim habit suits small gardens and container plantings. Plant in well-drained soil and avoid excessive shade, which fades the gold to green.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, sheltered soil.

directions: This compact hemlock has dense, glossy dark-green needles with silvery undersides. Site it out of drying wind and hot afternoon sun in cool, acidic soil. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist, as hemlocks resent drought.

planting.note: Plant in spring in humus-rich, well-drained soil.

directions: A graceful Japanese hemlock with lustrous foliage and a broad, spreading crown. It tolerates a bit more heat than northern species but still wants cool, moist roots. Provide shelter from wind and mulch generously to conserve soil moisture.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Notable for its resistance to the hemlock woolly adelgid, this species is gaining attention as a durable landscape tree. Plant in cool, moist, well-drained soil with some afternoon shade. It develops a full, pyramidal to rounded form as it matures.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in cool, sheltered soil.

directions: This mounding, weeping hemlock forms a broad cascade of layered, pendulous branches. Give it cool, moist, acidic soil and shelter from hot sun and wind. It grows slowly, so allow room for its eventual mounding spread when siting.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharply drained soil.

directions: A slow, drought-tolerant true cedar with short needles and a compact, conical crown. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and it will need little water once established. Avoid wet or heavy ground, which the species will not tolerate.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring; stake to set the desired height.

directions: This dramatic living sculpture has powder-blue needles on cascading, weeping branches trained over a staked leader. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and guide young growth to establish the framework you want. Once shaped it needs little care beyond occasional pruning of wayward shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest gold color.

directions: A graceful deodar with golden-yellow spring growth that softens to yellow-green in summer. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil to keep the color vivid and the habit open and pendulous. Protect from harsh winter wind at the cold edge of its range.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low, spreading groundcover conifer.

directions: A dwarf, spreading deodar with soft blue-green needles that stays low unless staked upward. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, where it drapes attractively over rocks and walls. Stake the leader if you want a small weeping tree instead of a groundcover.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with ample room to spread.

directions: This blue-needled form of the classic Lebanon cedar develops the same majestic, flat-topped tiers with age. Give it full sun, deep well-drained soil, and plenty of space for its wide horizontal branching. It is drought tolerant once established and long-lived.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A fast-growing deciduous conifer whose soft needles emerge blue-green and turn gold before dropping in autumn. Plant in full sun and moist, well-drained soil; it dislikes shade and drought. Its reddish winter twigs give good cold-season interest.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; extremely cold hardy.

directions: One of the hardiest of all trees, this larch thrives where winters are brutally cold and summers short. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, giving it plenty of light for straight, vigorous growth. Foliage turns clear yellow in fall before dropping.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cold, high-elevation gardens with gritty soil.

directions: A rugged high-mountain larch with woolly young shoots and brilliant golden fall color. It demands cold conditions, sharp drainage, and full sun, and struggles in warm lowland climates. Slow to establish, it rewards patient growers with alpine character.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates the coldest garden climates.

directions: This far-northern larch endures the harshest cold of any tree on earth and even grows over permafrost. Plant in full sun and moist, well-drained soil for the best growth. Its needles flush early in spring and glow golden before autumn leaf drop.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A vigorous hybrid of European and Japanese larch, valued in forestry for fast, disease-resistant growth. Plant in full sun and moist, well-drained soil where it quickly forms a straight, conical tree. Soft needles turn golden in fall before dropping.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring; stake to set cascade height.

directions: A weeping form of Japanese larch whose branches cascade to the ground from a staked leader. Plant in full sun and moist, well-drained soil, training the top to the height you prefer. The soft, deciduous needles turn gold in autumn for a striking seasonal display.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring; stake for the desired form.

directions: This pendulous European larch forms an irregular cascade whose shape depends on how it is trained and staked. Plant in full sun and moist, well-drained soil for vigorous growth. Its deciduous needles emerge bright green and turn butter-yellow before falling.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A stately Pacific Northwest timber tree with soft needles and distinctive cones bearing three-pointed bracts. Plant in full sun and deep, moist, well-drained soil, giving it ample room to reach its large size. It is also widely grown as a fragrant Christmas tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; hardier and more drought tolerant than the coastal type.

directions: The interior blue-needled variety is slower, more compact, and far more cold and drought tolerant than coastal Douglas fir. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, where it thrives in dry, continental climates. Its bluish foliage and dense habit suit windbreaks and screens.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharply drained soil.

directions: Native to Southern California's dry mountains, this species bears the largest cones of any Douglas fir. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and it withstands heat and drought once established. Its deep roots and thick bark make it well adapted to dry, fire-prone slopes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained, sheltered soil.

directions: A rare, smaller Douglas fir from Japan with notched needle tips and modest cones. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil with shelter from strong wind. It stays more compact than its North American relatives, suiting larger gardens and collections.

planting.note: Set out after the ground thaws so roots settle before summer heat.

directions: Plant the root ball level with the surrounding soil in a spot sheltered from harsh winter wind. Keep the soil evenly moist the first two seasons and mulch to buffer the shallow roots. The soft juvenile foliage flushes bronze-purple in cold weather and greens again in spring, which is the plant's signature seasonal show.

planting.note: Plant in spring so the compact rootball establishes before summer.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the container and twice as wide, then firm the soil gently around the neat rounded form. Water regularly through the first year and shear lightly only if you want to tighten the dome. Its dense mounding habit needs little pruning and turns a soft russet in winter.

planting.note: Space generously as a screen since this vigorous form fills in fast.

directions: Plant this fast-growing screening cultivar with the top of the rootball flush with grade in moist, well-drained ground. Give it deep watering during dry spells for the first few years while it builds its pyramidal frame. It holds better green winter color than the species, bronzing only slightly in the coldest weather.

planting.note: Give morning sun but shelter from scorching afternoon light to protect the pale tips.

directions: Site it where the new growth is protected from intense midday sun, which can bleach or burn the cream-yellow tips. Plant at grade in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water steadily while it establishes. The striking creamy-gold new shoots are brightest in spring against the older green interior.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot where the curled foliage can be viewed up close.

directions: Set the plant at grade in rich, evenly moist soil and keep it watered through establishment. Its needles spiral tightly around the twigs, so place it where visitors can admire the curled texture. Growth is slow and dense, needing only occasional shaping to keep a rounded outline.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its dark, irregular silhouette can stand out.

directions: Dig a wide hole and plant with the rootball level, then water deeply and mulch. New spring growth is light green and matures to an unusually deep, almost black-green as the season progresses. Its slow, dense, cone-shaped habit makes a bold specimen with minimal pruning.

planting.note: Tolerant of shade, so plant under high canopy where few conifers thrive.

directions: Plant this shade-loving conifer at grade in well-drained soil enriched with organic matter. It excels in deep or dappled shade where yews and hemlocks struggle, and it shrugs off deer browsing. Long, drooping dark-green needles give a soft look, and female plants bear olive-like seeds in autumn.

planting.note: Use as a columnar accent in shade where an upright yew-like form is wanted.

directions: Set the rootball level with the soil in a shaded or part-shaded bed with good drainage. Its stiff, upright branches carry whorls of dark needles in a bottlebrush pattern, forming a narrow vase over time. Heat- and deer-resistant, it needs only light tidying and occasional summer water.

planting.note: Plant as a low, wide groundcover for dry shade under trees.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained shade and space so the low, layered branches can knit into a wide mat. It thrives in dry shade and resists deer, making it useful under mature trees. Growth is slow, so give it room and water during droughts while it fills in.

planting.note: Give it a large open site as it becomes a massive symmetrical tree.

directions: Sow the large fresh seeds pointed-end down in deep pots, as the taproot resents disturbance, then transplant while young. Plant out in deep, well-drained soil in an open spot with room for the wide crown. The stiff, sharp, overlapping scale-leaves are the hallmark of this slow, long-lived tree.

planting.note: Frost-tender, so grow outdoors only in mild coastal climates or keep as a houseplant.

directions: In frost-free regions plant in deep, sandy, well-drained soil where the symmetrical tiered branches have room to spread. Grown indoors it wants bright light, even moisture, and turning to keep it straight. Its precise horizontal whorls of soft branchlets make it a popular living Christmas tree in pots.

planting.note: Needs a large frost-free site well away from paths due to heavy falling cones.

directions: Sow the large seeds warm and be patient, as they may form an underground tuber before sending up a shoot. Plant out in deep, fertile, well-drained ground with plenty of overhead space. Mature trees drop huge, heavy edible-seeded cones, so keep them away from seating and walkways.

planting.note: Strictly tropical to subtropical; needs a frost-free coastal setting.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil in a warm, frost-free location with full sun and shelter from cold wind. The tall, narrow, columnar crown characteristically leans toward the equator, a quirk that helps identify it. Water regularly while young; established trees tolerate salt spray and coastal exposure.

planting.note: Best in mild, subtropical gardens with cool winters but little hard frost.

directions: Sow the large, fresh seeds soon after ripening since they lose viability quickly, and plant seedlings out while young. Give it deep, moist but well-drained acidic soil in an open sunny site. The flat-topped candelabra crown and whorled branches of sharp needle-like leaves make it a striking specimen.

planting.note: Give a sheltered, frost-protected spot or grow in a large container to move indoors.

directions: Plant this living-fossil conifer in humus-rich, well-drained soil in a sheltered spot with dappled light while young. Protect it from hard frost, and in cold climates grow it in a big pot that can overwinter under cover. Its distinctive bubbly, chocolate-brown bark and multi-trunked, coppicing habit set it apart.

planting.note: Needs a mild, frost-free climate and deep, free-draining soil.

directions: Sow fresh seed warm and grow seedlings on in deep pots before planting into rich, well-drained, frost-free ground. Young trees have a conical form that broadens into a massive columnar trunk with age. Provide steady moisture while establishing; mature kauri are famously long-lived forest giants.

planting.note: A mild-climate evergreen useful as a screen, hedge, or patio tree.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade in a frost-free or nearly frost-free garden. The soft, fern-like blue-green foliage takes well to shearing, so it suits hedges, espaliers, and containers. Water regularly until established, after which it tolerates moderate drought and urban conditions.

planting.note: Tolerant of wind and coastal exposure in mild climates.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil in sun or part shade, choosing a spot sheltered from hard frost. Its stiff, sharp, olive-green needles and reddish, stringy bark give it a distinctive rugged look. It withstands wind and coastal salt and can be clipped into a dense hedge.

planting.note: The hardiest podocarp, good for rock gardens and low groundcover.

directions: Plant this low, spreading alpine conifer in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. Its tough, small bronze-green needles handle cold and wind, making it ideal for rockeries and troughs. Female plants set showy red fleshy seed receptacles in late summer when a male is nearby.

planting.note: Best in mild, moist gardens sheltered from cold drying winds.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil in a sheltered, partly shaded spot. The long, drooping, willow-like blue-green leaves give it a graceful, almost weeping texture. Keep it watered in dry spells and shield young plants from hard frost while they establish.

planting.note: A hardy, low, spreading shrub suited to rock gardens and containers.

directions: Set out in sharply drained soil in full sun where its low, wide-spreading branches can sprawl. The small needle-like leaves take on bronze and purple tints in cold weather, adding winter interest. It tolerates frost, wind, and poor ground once established, needing only occasional watering.

planting.note: A broadleaf-looking conifer for mild, subtropical gardens.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil in part shade in a warm, frost-free climate. Unusually for a conifer, it bears broad, oval, parallel-veined leaves that resemble a broadleaf tree. Water regularly while establishing; it makes a handsome, glossy-leaved shade or specimen tree in mild regions.

planting.note: Site in bright light but with some afternoon shade to prevent leaf scorch.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil where it gets good light but is shielded from harsh afternoon sun that can scorch the foliage. The soft needles emerge glowing golden-yellow, hold their color through summer, and turn coppery before autumn drop. Keep the root zone moist and mulched for best color and growth.

planting.note: Shelter from cold drying winds, which can brown the foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in a spot sheltered from harsh winter wind. Its broad, sharp, glossy lance-shaped needles spiral around the branches and often flush bronze in cold weather. It coppices readily, so cut stems regrow, and old foliage stays on the tree, adding texture.

planting.note: Needs a mild climate, ample moisture, and shelter while young.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, well-drained soil in a sheltered site in a mild climate. Young trees carry soft, blue-green, awl-shaped juvenile foliage that gives way to scale-like adult leaves with age. Water steadily during establishment; it grows into a tall, narrow, eventually massive forest conifer.

planting.note: Loves wet ground and pond edges in warm, humid climates.

directions: Plant this rare deciduous conifer in full sun in saturated or wet soil along water margins. Its soft, feathery foliage turns warm reddish-brown in autumn before falling. Keep the roots wet, as it is naturally a swamp tree and tolerates standing water well.

planting.note: Prefers cool, moist, humid conditions and dislikes summer heat.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, well-drained acidic soil in a humid, sheltered spot away from hot, dry exposure. The overlapping, incurved scale-like leaves give the branches a soft, cord-like texture. Growth is slow, so keep the soil consistently moist and protect young plants from drought and heat.

planting.note: Set out young palms after frost danger has passed so roots establish in warm soil.

directions: Sow fresh seed just under the surface in deep pots and keep warm and evenly moist until sprouting, which can take several months. Transplant to a sheltered, well-drained spot and mulch the root zone; its stiff, compact fronds resist wind far better than the standard windmill palm. Established plants tolerate hard frost once trunking begins.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost in a spot with good winter drainage.

directions: Start seed warm in a deep container and be patient, as germination is slow and uneven. Move to a sunny, sheltered site with fast-draining soil and water regularly through the first summers. This Himalayan species is among the cold-hardiest fan palms and shows a bare band of trunk below the crown as it ages.

planting.note: Set out in late spring once soil has warmed.

directions: Germinate fresh seed in warmth and pot on into rich, draining mix. Grow in full sun with steady summer moisture to develop its broad, flat, glossy leaf segments that give the palm its distinctive look. Give protection from hard freezes in marginal areas, as it is less cold-tolerant than most windmill palms.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sheltered location.

directions: Sow warm and keep the seed tray from drying out during the long germination period. Grow in full to part sun with generous water; the trunk is notably slender and clean with a partial fiber ring at the crown. It prefers milder winters and appreciates overhead protection where hard frosts occur.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost in a rock garden or well-drained bed.

directions: Start seed warm and grow the young palm in a lean, sharply draining mix. This trunkless species holds its fan leaves close to the ground and forms an underground stem, so plant at grade in a sunny spot. Water sparingly once established and protect from constant winter wet.

planting.note: Plant out in late spring in a bright, sheltered spot.

directions: Germinate fresh seed in a warm, humid environment and pot on carefully. Grow in full sun with good drainage to encourage the striking silvery-white underside of the leaves. A prized collector's windmill palm, it is best sited where its foliage backlights against a darker background.

common_name: Blue Mediterranean Fan Palm (Atlas Mountain Palm)

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and fast-draining soil.

directions: Soak and sow seed warm, allowing a couple of months for sprouting. This blue-gray form from the Atlas Mountains thrives in heat and lean, gritty soil and forms a slow, suckering clump. Water deeply but infrequently once established, and remove old leaf bases to show off the stout trunks.

planting.note: Set out in warm soil in spring; palmettos resent root disturbance when large.

directions: Sow warm and expect slow, strap-leaf seedlings that spend years building a root before trunking. Plant in full sun in any reasonable soil, including sandy coastal ground, as it tolerates salt spray well. Water through establishment, then let it grow on with little care into a stout-trunked fan palm.

planting.note: Plant in late spring where it has room for a massive trunk.

directions: Germinate seed in warmth and grow on in deep containers to accommodate the strong root system. Give it a large, sunny site because mature palms develop one of the thickest trunks in the genus. Water regularly while young, then reduce as the palm becomes drought-tolerant with age.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny, dry-summer location.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow the seedlings on the dry side to suit this desert species from northwestern Mexico. Site in full sun with excellent drainage, where its silvery blue-green fans stand out. Deep, infrequent watering during establishment produces a drought-hardy, heat-loving palm.

planting.note: Plant in late spring where a very large palm has room to grow.

directions: Start seed in warm conditions and grow on in deep pots. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil, giving generous room for its very tall trunk and large crown. Regular water speeds growth, though established plants handle drought and heat.

planting.note: Set out in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Clean and sow the seed warm, as germination is slow and staggered over many months. Grow in full sun in well-drained soil where its arching, blue-green feather leaves form a graceful crown. Named for the woolly covering on its flower sheath, it is cold-hardy and drought-tolerant once settled.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, open, well-drained site.

directions: Sow cleaned seed warm and allow a long, uneven germination. Grow in full sun in deep, well-drained soil; this South American species is taller than the common pindo palm and bears edible orange fruit. Deep watering in dry spells keeps growth steady, but mature palms are notably drought- and cold-hardy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with room for a broad, arching crown.

directions: This cross between pindo and queen palm is grown from the scarce hybrid seed, which germinates slowly, so most gardeners buy started plants. Site in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly for fast growth. It combines the lush look of a queen palm with much greater cold tolerance, making it a favorite for pushing tropical style into cooler zones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade or filtered light with rich, moist soil.

directions: Propagate most easily by dividing established clumps, or sow seed warm and be patient. Grow in shade or filtered light in humus-rich, evenly moist soil, protected from hot afternoon sun. Its finely divided, star-shaped fans are more delicate than lady palm, and it suckers slowly into an elegant understory clump.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Sow seed warm for reasonably quick germination and grow on in rich, draining mix. Plant in full sun or light shade in fertile soil with regular water, which suits this graceful cliff-dwelling date palm. Its glossy, gently arching leaves are softer and less stiff than most Phoenix, giving a lush, refined look.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Germinate seed warm and grow on in gritty, fast-draining soil. This clustering Mediterranean date palm from Crete handles heat, drought, and salt spray, and is hardier than the true date palm. Give it full sun and let established suckering clumps form a multi-trunked specimen.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to part shade.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow on in well-drained soil. This variable, often low-trunked Asian date palm tolerates a range of conditions and moderate cold. Plant in sun or part shade, water through establishment, and remove old fronds to reveal the stout, patterned trunk.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in full sun with fast drainage.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow on in a draining mix. This slender Baja fan palm reaches greater heights than the Mexican blue palm and carries green to lightly glaucous fans. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and water sparingly once established for a graceful, drought-tolerant landscape palm.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow the hard seed warm and be very patient, as germination and early growth are slow. Plant in full sun in deep, fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly to build the massive smooth gray trunk it becomes famous for. Extremely long-lived and cold-hardy, it is a majestic specimen palm for large landscapes.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in full sun to light shade with ample moisture.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow on in fertile, moist soil. This tall Australian fan palm tolerates damp ground and light shade when young, developing a slender ringed trunk and a dense glossy crown. Keep it well watered and it makes a stately avenue or specimen palm.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow slowly in a lean, gritty mix. This tough South American palm carries stiff, spiny blue-gray fans and tolerates cold, heat, and drought. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and water sparingly, handling the sharp leaf tips with care.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow on in fast-draining soil. This Brazilian palm forms a single trunk wrapped in a coarse spiny fiber and holds a crown of stiff green fans. Plant in full sun, water moderately while establishing, then treat as drought- and cold-tolerant.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in full sun with moist soil.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow on in moisture-retentive soil. This clustering fan palm from the Everglades tolerates wet ground and forms a thicket of slender trunks topped with green fans that show silvery undersides. Plant in full sun, keep it well watered, and feed regularly to avoid the yellowing it is prone to.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to part shade with rich, moist soil.

directions: Sow fresh seed warm and grow on in a humus-rich, moist mix. This shade-loving Mexican palm is usually trunkless but can slowly form a slender stem, and it is one of the cold-hardier Chamaedorea. Plant in shade, keep evenly moist, and enjoy the bright orange-red fruit clusters held near ground level on female plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to part shade with moist, rich soil.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow seedlings on in a shaded, humus-rich bed. This clustering, bamboo-like palm forms clumps of thin cane stems and tolerates light frost, making it useful for shady borders in mild climates. Keep soil evenly moist; female plants bear showy red fruit against the delicate foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with deep, well-drained soil in a cool, dry climate.

directions: Sow the large seed warm and be very patient through slow germination. This high-altitude Andean palm prefers cool, dry, Mediterranean-style climates rather than tropical heat and bears small coconut-like fruit. Plant in full sun with deep drainage and moderate water for a graceful, cold-tolerant feather palm.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in full sun in sandy, fast-draining soil.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow on in sandy, well-drained mix. This clumping Brazilian coastal palm stays low and trunkless, thriving in pure sand and heavy salt spray. Plant in full sun near the coast, water sparingly, and enjoy its arching plumose fronds and edible small fruit.

planting.note: Move outdoors only after nights stay reliably above 50F, or keep as a year-round houseplant.

directions: Set the crown at the same depth it grew in the nursery pot and use a rich, fast-draining mix. This palm is thirstier than most, so keep the rootball evenly moist and give it bright indirect light indoors or filtered sun outdoors. Feed through the warm months and expect airy arching fronds long before any flowering, which happens only on large mature specimens.

planting.note: Plant into warm soil once all frost danger has passed in zone 10 and up.

directions: Sow cleaned seed shallowly in a warm, humid propagator and expect slow, uneven sprouting. Plant young palms with the root flare at soil level in a spot with steady warmth and good drainage. Mature trees produce showy clusters of bright red fruit around the winter holidays, which gives the palm its name.

planting.note: Best kept as a lush houseplant or patio clump moved out after frost.

directions: This trunkless clumping palm is most easily increased by splitting an established clump into rooted divisions. Keep the mix consistently moist, since it naturally grows along streambanks and resents drying out. Provide bright filtered light and steady humidity for the fullest, greenest foliage.

planting.note: An ideal low-light houseplant; set outdoors only in warm shade after frost.

directions: Sow fresh seed warm and expect a slow, drawn-out germination typical of the genus. Prized for its blue-green fishtail leaves with a metallic sheen, it thrives in low to moderate light where many palms sulk. Keep it evenly moist, out of direct sun, and away from cold drafts.

planting.note: One of the hardiest feather palms, tolerating brief dips near 20F in a sheltered spot.

directions: Start seed in warmth, then grow young palms in shade until they are established. This clumping species forms slender bamboo-like canes and, unusually for the genus, sets bright orange-red fruit on female plants. Site it in dappled shade with some wind protection to push its cold tolerance in borderline zones.

planting.note: Tolerates light freezes into the low 20s, making it a favorite for shaded courtyards in warm-temperate zones.

directions: Fresh seed germinates fairly readily for a Chamaedorea if kept warm and moist. Most plants stay nearly trunkless with an elegant rosette of dark green fronds, and female plants carry showy red fruit at ground level. Grow in shade to part shade with steady moisture and sharp drainage.

planting.note: Needs warmth and moisture; grow under cover north of zone 10.

directions: Sow fresh seed warm and keep seedlings consistently moist in bright light. Young plants bear large, deeply divided circular fans, and the leaf stalks are armed with vicious curved orange spines, so handle with care. It appreciates rich, moist soil and full to filtered sun in a frost-free setting.

planting.note: Plant into warm ground after all frost risk in zone 10 and up.

directions: Sow fresh seed warm for prompt germination and grow the seedlings in bright light. The palm is named for the dense reddish-brown fuzzy fibers coating its crownshaft, which look like plush fur. Give it full sun, good drainage, and regular water through the warm growing season.

planting.note: A true tropical needing constant warmth, humidity, and shade; usually grown indoors or under glass.

directions: Sow fresh seed in a warm, humid propagator and expect slow, uneven emergence. This slow-growing palm is prized for its nearly circular, pleated undivided leaves with saw-toothed edges. It demands deep shade, high humidity, warmth, and steadily moist soil to look its best.

planting.note: One of the hardiest palms, surviving into the mid-teens in a sheltered spot.

directions: Soak seed and sow warm, then grow the tough seedlings on in full sun. This clumping Mediterranean fan palm forms multiple trunks and tolerates heat, drought, and coastal exposure once established. Watch for the sharp spines along the leaf stalks when working around it.

planting.note: Tropical clumper best grown indoors or under cover outside zone 10.

directions: Sow fresh seed warm and keep the seedlings humid and evenly moist. This clustering palm forms multiple slender green-ringed trunks and a full head of arching fronds, making a lush screen or container specimen. Give it bright filtered light, steady water, and warmth year-round.

planting.note: Single-trunked tropical needing warmth; grow indoors north of zone 10.

directions: Sow fresh seed warm and grow seedlings in bright, filtered light. Unlike its clustering relative, this palm forms a single slim trunk topped by a green crownshaft and a neat crown of arching fronds. Keep it warm, sheltered, and evenly watered, avoiding cold drafts and drought.

planting.note: Set out only once nights stay reliably above the mid-fifties.

directions: Sow the fat orange seeds on their side and barely cover them with gritty mix, keeping it warm and lightly moist. Grow in a fast-draining bed with midday shade in the hottest climates, and water only when the top few inches dry. A fresh whorl of arching, ferny leaves flushing all at once signals a healthy plant; expect years before the first cone appears.

planting.note: One of the more cold-tolerant sagos, safe to plant after the last hard frost.

directions: Press cleaned seeds half into a warm sand-and-perlite bed and wait patiently, as sprouting is uneven. This robust Taiwanese cousin of the common sago wants full sun, sharp drainage, and a deep soak followed by a dry-down. Its flush of stiff, glossy fronds is larger and faster than a King Sago, and mature plants shrug off light frost.

planting.note: Among the hardiest cycads, tolerating brief dips near the low twenties once established.

directions: Germinate seed warm in a gritty mix and be prepared to wait several months. This Chinese species is prized for cold tolerance, so give it a sunny, sharply drained spot and let it dry between waterings. New leaves emerge silvery-blue before hardening to green; keep it lean and slightly dry to bring out the color.

planting.note: Strictly tropical; plant only where frost never occurs.

directions: Sow the large buoyant seeds warm and humid, giving them steady bottom heat. This fast, tall coastal species enjoys rich, moist, well-drained soil and regular water in the growing season. Give it room, since the trunk lifts high and the crown of glossy fronds spreads wide; protect it entirely from cold.

planting.note: A warm-climate cycad best planted at the start of the wet, warm season.

directions: Start the reddish seeds in a warm, free-draining medium and expect slow, staggered germination. This tropical Australian cycad thrives in full sun with a defined wet-and-dry rhythm, so water generously through the warm months and ease off when cool. Watch for the tall single flush of bright green fronds that marks each growth cycle.

planting.note: A collectible Chinese species happiest planted in warm spring soil.

directions: Germinate fresh seed warm and be very patient, as this rare cycad can take many months to sprout. Give it a bright, sheltered spot with fast-draining soil and steady but not soggy moisture. Its distinctive three- to four-times divided, feathery leaves are the draw; protect the delicate new flush from wind and hard cold.

planting.note: A small tropical zamia for frost-free gardens or pots.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in a warm, humid propagator and keep the mix lightly moist. This dainty Mexican zamia stays small, making it ideal for shaded understory plantings or containers with excellent drainage. Its soft, papery leaflets flush pinkish before turning green; shelter it from direct hot sun and any frost.

planting.note: A compact, fast-growing zamia for warm shade gardens.

directions: Start seed warm and moist; this species is unusually quick to sprout and grow for a cycad. Plant in dappled shade with rich, well-drained soil and keep it evenly watered through the growing season. The lush flushes of thin, glossy leaflets appear frequently on happy plants, but any cold snap will damage the foliage.

planting.note: A tough Mexican zamia for warm, frost-free sites.

directions: Germinate cleaned seed in a warm, gritty mix kept just moist. This adaptable zamia takes sun or part shade and tolerates dry spells once its swollen underground stem is established. Look for narrow, often toothed leaflets on upright fronds; ease back on water in cool weather to avoid rot.

planting.note: A tall tropical dioon best planted in warm, settled weather.

directions: Sow the large seeds warm and be patient, as dioons germinate slowly and unevenly. Give this stately, tall-trunked species rich, well-drained soil, some protection from harsh sun, and regular water in the growing season. It produces one of the largest crowns of any dioon; the arching flush of spine-tipped leaflets is the signature feature.

planting.note: A robust Central American dioon for warm, frost-free gardens.

directions: Start the large seeds warm in a deep, free-draining container and expect months of waiting. This handsome dioon takes full sun in humid climates and rewards steady watering with fast, strong growth for its genus. Its stiff, upright then arching fronds emerge in a single flush; shield young plants from cold and drying wind.

planting.note: A drought-tolerant Mexican dioon for warm, sunny sites.

directions: Germinate seed warm in a gritty, fast-draining mix and allow several months. This mountain species handles full sun, heat, and dry spells once established, so water sparingly and never let it sit wet. Its blue-green, keeled leaflets and compact crown make it a striking, tough landscape cycad.

planting.note: A high-elevation Mexican dioon with good tolerance of cool nights.

directions: Sow seed warm and keep the medium barely moist during the long germination period. Give this stout dioon full sun and very sharp drainage, watering only after the soil dries well down. Its dense crown of stiff, gray-green fronds tolerates heat and cool nights alike, but young plants dislike prolonged wet cold.

planting.note: A coastal African cycad for warm, nearly frost-free gardens.

directions: Sow cleaned seed on warm, moist sand and give it bottom heat for even germination. This popular Encephalartos takes sun or light shade in sandy, well-drained soil and appreciates regular water in warm weather. Its glossy, stiffly toothed leaves are ornamental year-round, and mature plants push spectacular bright coral-red cones.

planting.note: A drought- and cold-tolerant blue cycad for hot, dry gardens.

directions: Germinate seed warm in a lean, gritty mix and keep it barely damp. This sculptural species demands full sun and razor-sharp drainage to develop its intense silver-blue, viciously spiny leaves. Water sparingly, let it dry hard between soakings, and expect stronger blue color under bright, dry conditions.

planting.note: A blue-leaved, arid-climate cycad tolerant of light frost.

directions: Start seed warm and keep the medium on the dry side to avoid rot. This tough South African cycad wants full sun and fast-draining, gritty soil, thriving on neglect once established. Its broad, powder-blue fronds with smooth-edged leaflets are the highlight; overwatering fades the color and invites disease.

planting.note: A large, long-lived cycad for spacious warm-temperate gardens.

directions: Sow the large seeds warm and be prepared for slow, uneven sprouting. This vigorous Eastern Cape giant takes full sun to light shade in deep, well-drained soil and grows steadily into a substantial trunked specimen. Give it room to spread its long, glossy fronds; mature plants form massive green cones.

planting.note: A big, fast-growing cycad for large frost-light gardens.

directions: Germinate the large seeds in warm sand with steady bottom heat. This impressive species is relatively quick for an Encephalartos, thriving in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and regular summer water. Its long, arching dark-green fronds form a broad crown atop a stout trunk; protect it from hard freezes.

planting.note: Known only from cloned offsets since no female plant exists; propagate by suckers.

directions: Because only male clones survive, this legendary cycad is grown from rooted basal offsets rather than seed. Detach a firm pup, let the cut callus, then pot it in a warm, very well-drained mix until roots form. Plant out in full sun with rich soil and steady warm-season water; its glossy, arching golden-green fronds are the reward.

planting.note: A shade-loving, near-trunkless cycad for woodland gardens.

directions: Sow seed warm and moist for reasonably reliable germination. Unusual among the genus, this species prefers dappled shade and moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil. It stays largely trunkless underground while throwing long, arching, glossy dark-green fronds; keep it out of hot direct sun for the best foliage.

planting.note: One of the tallest African cycads, for large warm gardens.

directions: Germinate the large seeds warm and keep them lightly moist during the long wait. This towering species grows relatively fast in full sun with deep, rich, well-drained soil and generous summer water. It develops a tall trunk crowned with long glossy fronds and produces very large golden-yellow cones on mature plants.

planting.note: A hardy, adaptable Eastern Cape cycad for sunny sites.

directions: Sow seed in a warm, gritty mix and allow several months for sprouting. This durable species tolerates a range of conditions but prefers full sun and sharply drained soil, needing little water once established. Its long, straight, dark green to slightly bluish fronds form a stately crown; avoid soggy soil in cool weather.

planting.note: A shade-loving Mexican cycad for mild, frost-light gardens.

directions: Sow seed warm and just covered in a moist, well-drained mix. This graceful cycad wants filtered shade, humus-rich soil, and steady moisture, resenting hot direct sun. Its leaflets are clustered in bamboo-like groups along the stalk, and new flushes emerge coppery before greening; shelter it from hard frost.

planting.note: A forest-floor cycad for warm, shaded, frost-light gardens.

directions: Germinate seed warm in a moist, free-draining medium under humid cover. This adaptable cycad thrives in dappled shade with rich, evenly moist soil and gentle protection from wind. Its arching fronds flush bronze-red before hardening to deep green, and the cones bear the paired horns that give the genus its name.

planting.note: A large tropical cycad for humid, frost-free gardens.

directions: Sow the seeds warm and humid and keep the mix consistently moist during germination. This is the largest Ceratozamia, wanting light shade, fertile well-drained soil, and generous water in the warm season. Its long, broad-leafleted fronds flush reddish and arch outward into a wide crown; it needs a strictly frost-free spot.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once the ground has warmed and hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant the rhizome mass level with the soil surface in rich, well-drained ground and water deeply until established. Install a root barrier or trench annually to keep this vigorous runner in bounds. New culms shoot up in spring, often with a distinctive yellow groove and occasional zigzag base; thin older canes each year to show off the coloring.

planting.note: Plant in spring so canes have a full season to root before winter.

directions: Site divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and mulch to conserve water while roots take hold. This selection has bright golden culms striped with a contrasting green groove that intensifies in full sun. Contain the running rhizomes with a barrier, and remove crowded or weak canes in late winter to feature the vivid coloring.

planting.note: Spring planting suits this cold-hardy species best.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions at soil level and keep evenly moist during the first two seasons. One of the toughest and most cold-tolerant running bamboos, it makes a fast, dense evergreen screen. Restrain the spreading roots with a barrier and thin older culms in spring to encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Set out in spring to give shoots a full growing season.

directions: Plant divisions level with the ground in deep, fertile soil and water regularly while establishing. Prized for both cold hardiness and tender edible spring shoots, the new culms carry a whitish bloom and often a purplish cast. Harvest emerging shoots when a few inches tall, or leave them to grow and thin the grove each year.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Plant the divided clump at grade in well-drained soil and irrigate through the establishment period. This adaptable timber-type runner produces tall, straight culms and good-quality edible shoots. Contain the aggressive rhizomes with a barrier and remove older canes annually to keep the grove open.

planting.note: Spring is the ideal time to establish new divisions.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level in fertile ground and keep well watered as they root. The thick-walled culms give off a sandalwood-like scent when rubbed, and the species tolerates wet, heavy soils better than most bamboos. Barrier the running rhizomes and thin the grove each spring to display the sturdy canes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered location for best size.

directions: Plant the rhizome division level with the soil in deep, rich ground and irrigate generously during establishment. Known as Madake, this is one of the largest and most useful timber bamboos, needing several years and warm summers to reach full stature. Contain the vigorous runners and remove older culms selectively to harvest strong, straight canes.

planting.note: Set out in spring to allow a full season of establishment.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and water deeply through the first summers. This large gray-green form makes an imposing grove of tall, arching culms. Install a rhizome barrier to control the running roots and thin crowded canes in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring for the strongest first-year growth.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in well-drained ground and keep evenly moist while rooting. New culms emerge with a striking silvery-blue waxy bloom that gradually fades to green. Contain the runners with a barrier and thin the grove yearly to spotlight the fresh, powdery shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the soil warms.

directions: Set divisions at soil level in fertile ground and water regularly during establishment. The lower internodes often kink into a gentle zigzag, and mature culms may darken toward black in strong light. Barrier the rhizomes and remove older canes each spring to keep the grove tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring for reliable establishment.

directions: Plant the rhizome mass at grade in well-drained soil and keep watered while it settles in. A fast, adaptable running species valued for quick screening and good cold tolerance. Contain the roots with a barrier and thin older culms annually to maintain an open grove.

planting.note: Plant in spring, ideally near a damp or low-lying site.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level in moist, even boggy ground where most bamboos would fail. This species has air canals in its roots that let it thrive in wet conditions and along pond edges. Keep the soil damp, barrier the runners, and thin canes in spring to renew the stand.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to develop the best color.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in rich, well-drained ground and water deeply while rooting. This showy selection has thick golden-yellow culms streaked with random green stripes, brightest in full sun. Barrier the vigorous rhizomes and thin older canes each spring to feature the colorful stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a full season of shoot growth.

directions: Plant the division at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and irrigate regularly to establish. Noted for very fast, thick new shoots and prominent nodes, it grows to timber size in favorable sites. Contain the running roots with a barrier and thin the grove annually.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in rich ground and keep well watered during establishment. Grown in China for its excellent, sweet edible shoots, it also forms a handsome upright grove. Barrier the rhizomes, harvest young spring shoots as they emerge, and thin remaining culms to size up the stand.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Plant the clump at soil level in cool, moist, humus-rich ground and mulch well. This non-invasive clumper forms a graceful fountain of fine, dark, purplish culms and delicate foliage, disliking hot afternoon sun. Water steadily in dry spells and simply remove any weak or dead canes at the base.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot with some shade.

directions: Plant the divided clump at grade in fertile, moist, well-drained soil and keep watered while establishing. An upright, well-behaved clumper, it makes a fast evergreen screen with striking white-banded new culm sheaths. Give it shade from harsh afternoon sun and thin out any weak canes at the base.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates more sun than most Fargesia.

directions: Plant the clump level with the soil in moist, well-drained ground and mulch to keep roots cool. This compact, heat-tolerant clumper forms a tidy arching mound and is a favorite hedge and container bamboo. Water regularly, especially in pots, and remove old canes at the base to refresh the clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, partly shaded position.

directions: Plant the division at soil level in rich, moisture-retentive ground and keep it evenly watered. A graceful, cold-hardy clumper with arching culms and lush foliage that provides good winter cover for wildlife. Shelter it from strong afternoon sun and drying wind, and cut away any spent canes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the clump at grade in moist, humus-rich soil and mulch to hold moisture. Fresh culms emerge blue-green with orange-red sheaths, aging to olive tones for a colorful, upright clumping screen. Give some afternoon shade in hot regions, water in dry spells, and thin weak canes at the base.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, sheltered site.

directions: Plant the division level with the soil in moist, well-drained, humus-rich ground and mulch well. A hardy, elegant clumper with slender arching culms and small leaves, well suited to cool woodland edges. Keep it watered and shaded from hot sun, and remove any dead or crowded canes at the base.

planting.note: Plant in the warm season in frost-free climates only.

directions: Plant rooted culm cuttings or divisions in warm, fertile soil and water deeply while establishing. This large tropical clumper is one of the most widely grown bamboos, sensitive to hard frost but very vigorous in heat. Give it room, feed and water in the growing season, and thin older culms to open the clump.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in a frost-free garden.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in warm, rich soil and keep well watered during establishment. This ornamental form has brilliant golden culms boldly striped with green, one of the showiest tropical clumping bamboos. Protect from frost, feed through summer, and remove crowded or damaged canes to display the color.

planting.note: Plant in the warm season where winters are mild.

directions: Plant divisions or cuttings in warm, well-drained soil and irrigate while roots establish. A tall, upright, clumping bamboo whose straight thin-walled culms are traditionally used for weaving and make an elegant screen. Modestly frost-tolerant, it appreciates feeding and steady moisture through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in the warm season in mild-winter areas.

directions: Plant the division in warm, fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly to establish. This narrow, non-invasive clumper is a top choice for tight privacy screens, holding its foliage from the ground up. Feed and water through summer and remove weak or leaning canes to keep the column dense.

planting.note: Plant in the warm season in mild climates or grow in a container.

directions: Plant the division in warm, well-drained soil or a large pot and keep evenly watered. This compact clumper forms a dense fountain of golden culms and fine foliage, ideal for hedges and containers. Give full sun for the brightest color, feed in summer, and trim to shape as desired.

planting.note: Plant in the warm season in a frost-free spot.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in warm, fertile soil and irrigate deeply while establishing. A strong, upright tropical clumper with tough culms traditionally used as boat poles and for construction. Protect from frost, feed and water through the growing season, and thin older canes to keep the clump open.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade for the brightest variegation.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level in fertile, moist ground and water while establishing. This low, spreading dwarf has vivid golden leaves striped with green, best when cut to the ground in early spring for fresh, brightly colored regrowth. Contain the runners with a barrier or grow it in a bed edged by pavement.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or part shade.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in moist, fertile ground and keep watered. This compact running dwarf has crisp cream-and-green striped foliage that makes a bright groundcover or container plant. Cut it low in early spring for the freshest variegation and contain the spreading rhizomes.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a groundcover in sun or part shade.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in moist, well-drained soil and water until established. One of the smallest bamboos, it forms a dense, low mat useful as a lawn substitute or erosion cover. Mow or shear it to a few inches in late winter for tidy new growth, and confine the vigorous runners with a barrier.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to full sun.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in moist, fertile ground and keep watered while rooting. This tidy dwarf carries small fern-like leaves in flat, two-ranked sprays, making a fine low hedge or groundcover. Shear it low in early spring for compact fresh growth and barrier the running roots.

planting.note: Plant in spring for an upright screen.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground and water regularly to establish. A tall, erect running bamboo with strong culms that forms a dense hedge or windbreak. Contain the spreading rhizomes with a barrier and thin older canes each spring to keep the stand vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a contained bed in sun or shade.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in moist, rich ground and keep well watered. This bold, tropical-looking runner has very large, broad leaves that fan out from the tops of its culms, thriving in shade and damp soil. It spreads aggressively, so always confine it with a deep barrier or a paved edge.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a contained shady bed.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level in moist, humus-rich ground and water while establishing. Grown for its striking winter effect, the broad green leaves dry to pale straw-colored margins that look like variegation. It runs aggressively and needs firm containment; cut the whole planting low in early spring to renew the foliage.
